original_abstract: Cyclic molecular architectures, particularly benzannulated medium-sized and macrocyclic ring (BMR) systems, are essential structural motifs found in a vast array of biologically active natural products and synthetic compounds. These scaffolds provide critical conformational constraints and structural pre-organization, which often lead to enhanced binding affinity, selectivity, and improved bioavailability for challenging protein targets. Given their therapeutic significance, several BMR-based molecules have successfully transitioned into clinical use as drugs or are currently navigating late-stage clinical trials, particularly in the field of oncology. This review highlights the pharmacological importance of BMRs and examines the robust synthetic methodologies developed for their construction—including cyclization strategies such as ring-closing metathesis, transition metal-catalyzed couplings, and carbohydrate-templated approaches—aiming to bridge the gap between synthetic complexity and drug discovery.
humanized_abstract: Cyclic molecules characterized by benzannulated medium-sized and macrocyclic ring (BMR) structures are prevalent in the structure of biologically active natural compounds and synthetic analogs. Frequently, such cyclic constraints and pre-organized structures impart substantial affinity and selectivity towards target proteins while facilitating the bioavailability of the compounds, rendering them of extraordinary pharmacological importance. Several BMRs-derived small molecule ligands have entered clinical trials and are on the market for use as drugs and/or have reached advanced stages of clinical trials in various therapeutic areas such as oncology. This review covers in a unifying fashion the pharmacological properties of BMR systems and corresponding advances in synthetic methodologies devised to obtain them such as, but not limited to, ring closing metathesis and transition metal mediated cross-coupling reactions, as well as carbohydrate-templated approaches, looking to bridge the essential challenge for the preclinical drug development process.
